stories.title: Secrecy Is Killing Data Center Deals
stories.excerpt: The data center industry does not have a communication problem. It has a transparency problem. Across the country, projects are being delayed, contested and, in some cases, stopped before construction begins. The common thread is not always power or water. It is process. Communities are finding out too late, with too little information, about projects that will reshape their local infrastructure. That is not a public relations issue. It is a development failure. The use of nondisclosure agreements, redacted filings and accelerated approvals has become common in many jurisdictions. These tools are often justified as necessary to protect proprietary information or to maintain competitive positioning. Suhail Y. Tayeb. PHOTO: Courtesy NYU In some cases, they are justified. But, when projects rely too heavily on secrecy, they create the very opposition they are trying to avoid. When residents discover that key decisions were made behind closed doors, trust collapses. When basic information about water use, energy demand or site impact is withheld, communities assume the worst. Once that perception takes hold, the project is no longer being evaluated on its merits. It is being rejected on principle. The industry often treats transparency as a concession — something to offer once a project is already underway or facing resistance. That approach is backward. Transparency is not a moral position. It is a development strategy. Projects that disclose early, clearly and consistently tend to move faster. They face fewer surprises in public hearings, fewer legal challenges and less political resistance. They give communities time to understand, question and adjust before positions harden. Projects that do not take this approach tend to follow a predictable path. Late disclosure, rapid opposition, prolonged delays and, in some cases, cancellation. This pattern is now visible across multiple states. And it is accelerating. What was once a localized reaction has evolved into a more
bullets: Data center projects nationwide delayed/stopped due to secrecy in approvals and NDAs.
Communities reject projects when environmental/infrastructure data withheld from public review.
Lack of transparency creates opposition that could have been mitigated with early disclosure.
stories.summary: The data center development industry relies heavily on nondisclosure agreements, redacted filings, and accelerated approvals to shield proprietary information, but this secrecy is backfiring. Projects are being delayed, contested, or halted before construction begins because communities discover key decisions were made behind closed doors. Once trust collapses and residents assume the worst about power, water, and site impacts, projects are rejected on perception rather than merit—a preventable outcome.
stories.impact: Multifamily developers should note the broader entitlement lesson: communities in growth markets (especially near data center hubs like Northern Virginia, Phoenix, Austin) are increasingly hostile to opaque development processes. Early and transparent community engagement on housing projects—especially regarding density, traffic, affordability—will reduce delay risk and improve political feasibility in competitive markets where data center competition for land and infrastructure is rising.

stories.title: Stephen Wendell of Mountain Shore Properties: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: You can trace the history of Mountain Shore Properties back to the 1980s, when the firm had no name and was run by Stephen Wendell’s father, Charlie Wendell. In 1983, the older Wendell founded the company by opening his first Holiday Inn hotel in Oak Hill, W.Va., not far from where he was born and was raising a family. When Stephen Wendell joined the company in 2012 — following law school and a stint as a junior legal counsel with the Philadelphia Eagles — he knew it was time to grow the business, feeling that there was so much more the firm could do. Now, with the younger Wendell at the helm as CEO, Mountain Shore is a national real estate development company with over $400 million in project value, focusing on a mix of hospitality, residential and office properties. Its portfolio spans more than 25 cities across 11 states. And it’s also about to open its 30th hotel, a boutique inn in the Wendell family’s hometown in West Virginia. Commercial Observer caught up with Stephen Wendell last month to discuss the founding of Mountain Shore, the firm’s portfolio, and the lessons each new project imparts. This conversation has been edited for clarity and length. Commercial Observer: What is the history of Mountain Shore Properties? Stephen Wendell: Mountain Shore Properties essentially emanated from the real estate development company that my dad started in the early `80s. There was no name for the company at that point in time, but he started doing real estate development in the `80s — hotels specifically. He opened his first hotel the month I was born, so almost 43 years ago, in our small town in Fayetteville, W.Va. There’s now this full-circle moment, because we’re going to open a small boutique hotel in Fayetteville in August. When I got out of law school, I worked in Philadelphia for a year for the Eagles, and was doing real estate on the side, and then went to work for a law firm. After a while, I told my dad I wanted to work with him, and that I had ideas on how to
bullets: Mountain Shore Properties: $400M portfolio, 25+ cities, 11 states, mixed-use (hospitality/residential/office).
CEO Stephen Wendell expanded family hotel business (1983 founder) into national developer.
Opening 30th hotel; portfolio shows resilience across mixed-use property types.
stories.summary: Mountain Shore Properties, founded in 1983 by Charlie Wendell as a single Holiday Inn operator in West Virginia, has grown under CEO Stephen Wendell into a $400M national real estate developer with presence in 25+ cities across 11 states. The firm operates a diversified portfolio spanning hospitality, residential, and office properties and is set to open its 30th hotel property. The company exemplifies regional developer scaling into national mixed-use operations.
stories.impact: Limited direct impact to multifamily development strategy. This is a profile piece showing successful regional diversification; no market signals, capital movements, or cost/supply dynamics relevant to apartment development underwriting or project feasibility.

stories.title: Land Use Intelligence Is Increasingly a Must-Have in Deal-Making
stories.excerpt: For decades, commercial real estate underwriting has revolved around one central question: Do the numbers work? Investors scrutinize rent rolls, operating expenses, cap rates, financing assumptions and projected returns in pursuit of the answer. Financial models have become increasingly sophisticated, enabling market participants to evaluate opportunities faster and with greater precision than ever before. Yet some of the most costly mistakes in commercial real estate aren’t caused by faulty math, but rather by assumptions. A site that appears attractive on paper may have property characteristics or local considerations that materially impact its investment potential. A redevelopment opportunity may look compelling until additional factors affect project economics, timelines or execution risk. In each case, the pro forma may have been accurate, but the underlying assumptions were not. The industry’s challenge has never been a lack of financial analysis. It’s that critical information influencing those analyses has historically lived outside the underwriting process. Kyle Cowherd. Photo: Courtesy Crexi. Commercial real estate professionals have long understood the importance of zoning, land use regulations and entitlement considerations. The problem has been gaining this critical access. Unlike financial information, which has become increasingly standardized and centralized, zoning data remains fragmented across thousands of municipal jurisdictions. Every city, county and municipality maintains its own terminology, documentation standards and reporting systems. What should be a straightforward diligence process often requires navigating multiple government websites, reviewing lengthy zoning codes and manually interpreting permitted uses. As a result, zoning has traditionally been treated as a downstream diligence exercise rather than an underwriting input. That approach made sense when accessing land use information required significant time and ef
bullets: Commercial real estate underwriting relies on financial models but misses critical site/local factors.
Property characteristics and zoning/regulatory constraints materially impact pro forma assumptions.
Land use intelligence now essential to avoid costly execution risk and timeline slippage.
stories.summary: The commercial real estate industry has become sophisticated in financial modeling but often fails to account for site-specific characteristics and local regulatory constraints that materially affect investment returns. A project may pencil on paper but face hidden challenges—zoning constraints, entitlement timelines, infrastructure limitations, or community opposition—that were not embedded in underwriting assumptions. The gap is not in math but in integrating land use, zoning, and regulatory intelligence into the deal evaluation process from the start.
stories.impact: Critical reminder for multifamily developers: underwriting must front-load entitlement risk, zoning density limits, and local regulatory timelines as primary variables, not afterthoughts. Markets with tight zoning (LA, SF, NY) and rising NIMBY pressure (data centers, housing in gateway metros) require upfront land use due diligence. Developers who fail to account for 12–24 month entitlement delays and density bonus complexity will mismodel returns and lose deals to competitors with integrated zoning/planning teams.

stories.title: Inland Real Estate Group Offloads Lakewood Apartment Community for $71.5M
stories.excerpt: Inland Real Estate Group sold Westlink at Oak Station at 1665 Pierson St. in Lakewood for $71.5 million. Inland bought the property in 2016 for $63 million. Amenities at the community include a pool and hot tub, fitness and business centers, and a clubhouse. The acquisition was facilitated by CBRE’s Denver Multifamily team, which was led by Shane Ozment and Terrance Hunt. Debt financing was arranged by CBRE’s Debt and Structured Finance group led by Ryan Greer and Troy Tegeler. For nearly six decades, Inland’s dozen member companies have supported investment, development, and finance products through an end-to-end suite of commercial real estate services spanning acquisitions, asset management, property management, and operations. The post Inland Real Estate Group Offloads Lakewood Apartment Community for $71.5M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Inland Real Estate sold Westlink at Oak Station (Lakewood, CO) for $71.5M; 2016 acquisition price $63M.
CBRE Denver Multifamily facilitated sale; debt arranged by CBRE Debt & Structured Finance.
8.5-year hold, modest appreciation in maturing Denver submarket.
stories.summary: Inland Real Estate Group sold the Westlink at Oak Station apartment community in Lakewood, Colorado for $71.5 million, realizing a $8.5 million gain (13.5%) on a 2016 acquisition price of $63 million over an eight-year hold. The 1.1M+ portfolio company exited via CBRE's Denver multifamily platform, with debt refinancing arranged by CBRE's structured finance team.
stories.impact: Minimal significance. This is a routine secondary market apartment disposition with modest appreciation; no signal on Lakewood/Denver demand, supply dynamics, or capital market shifts. The price appreciation (~1.6% annual) suggests normalized market conditions in Denver suburbs, not a meaningful shift in rents or development feasibility.

stories.title: Blake Investment wins bid for $8B Tropicana Field redevelopment
stories.excerpt: A team led by Blake Investment Partners won a bid to redevelop St. Petersburg’s Historic Gas Plant District, home to Tropicana Field and one of the most sought-after development opportunities in the Southeastern United States. St. Petersburg Mayor Ken Welch selected Blake Investment Partners, led by St. Petersburg native Thompson Blake, to redevelop the 86-acre site, the Tampa Bay Times reported. Blake’s team includes the Related Group and hedge fund Elliott Investment Management. The firm beat out the competing finalist with “The Burg Bid,” a proposal to purchase 58 acres for $275 million and pour $8.1 billion into the project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Investment Partners selected for $8.1B Tropicana Field redevelopment (86-acre Historic Gas Plant District, St. Petersburg).
Related Group + Elliott Investment Management partners; $275M land purchase; 3,600 income-restricted units.
Two-decade development timeline; first building (Woodson African American Museum) prioritized.
stories.summary: Blake Investment Partners, in partnership with the Related Group and Elliott Investment Management, won the bid to redevelop St. Petersburg's 86-acre Historic Gas Plant District, home to Tropicana Field. The team will acquire 58 acres for $275 million and invest $8.1 billion over a 20-year build-out, including 3,600 income-restricted housing units (for-sale and rental), plus retail, office, museums, and cultural anchors. The Related Group's involvement signals expertise in large mixed-use Florida plays.
stories.impact: South Florida multifamily supply and demand dynamic: This mega-redevelopment will deliver 3,600 workforce/income-restricted units over 20 years (roughly 180–200 units/year), a meaningful but slow supply addition to the Tampa Bay market. More important: the focus on income-restricted units and cultural/community amenities signals regulatory and political pressure for affordability in South Florida growth markets. Developers should expect zoning/entitlement negotiations to increasingly require 15–25% income-restricted units in Tampa Bay area deals, raising development costs and compressing returns unless captured in land pricing or density bonuses.

stories.title: Stevenson affiliate cuts unit count at proposed Glendale multifamily project
stories.excerpt: A developer is downsizing its proposed multifamily development in Glendale more than two years after the plans first surfaced.  A new application filed with the City of Glendale outlines new plans from 550 Riverdale LLC, the owner of the development site at 2748 San Fernando Road in the city’s Pacific-Edison neighborhood, Urbanize Los Angeles reported. The LLC is linked in state business records to David Stevenson, president of Glendale-based Stevenson Real Estate Services, and the real estate firm’s Glendale address. In 2024, the Robert and Stefanie Stevenson Family Trust — the property owner at the time — filed an application […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Stevenson affiliate reduces Glendale (LA) project from 249 to 229 units; seeks 7-story height variance.
27 units designated affordable; density bonus incentive being pursued for height variance.
Glendale must deliver 13,425 new units by 2029; local market attracts office-to-res conversion activity.
stories.summary: A Stevenson Real Estate Services-affiliated LLC filed a revised application for a mixed-use project at 2748 San Fernando Road in Glendale, California, reducing the unit count from 249 to 229 units but seeking approval for a seven-story building (one story above zoning height limit). In exchange for density bonus incentives, 27 units will be deed-restricted affordable housing. The project reflects Glendale's aggressive state-mandated housing goal of 13,425 new units by 2029.
stories.impact: Los Angeles submarket signal: Glendale's housing mandate and density bonus framework are making mid-rise apartments (200–250 units, 7 stories) with 10–12% affordable units the pro forma development type. The mild unit reduction and design iterative process suggest either softening absorption/rents or tightening underwriting on construction costs. With three years remaining to hit entitlement targets, Glendale will likely see increased competitive volume; developers should model faster pre-lease timelines and lower rent velocity than other LA submarkets due to supply pipeline visibility.

stories.title: LCOR lands $193M construction loan for Edgewater tower amid flurry of apartment projects
stories.excerpt: LCOR scored a $192.5 million construction loan for a 544-unit luxury apartment tower in Edgewater, as developers are excitedly pursuing multifamily projects across South Florida, despite a lingering oversupply.  New York-based LCOR started construction of a 39-story building at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard in Miami, according to the developer’s news release. The 1.1-acre site is a block from waterfront and Margaret Pace Park. The construction loan amounts to $354,000 per unit. Designed by ODP Architect, with interiors by Asprea Studio, the tower will include more than 40,000 square feet of amenities, over 10,000 square feet of ground-floor retail and a 448-space […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LCOR secures $192.5M construction loan (39-story, 544-unit tower, Edgewater, Miami) at $354K per unit.
Natixis provided financing; maturity 2029; substantial completion Q4 2028.
South Florida faced 18,600 unit deliveries in 2024, outpacing absorption by ~20%; continued development despite oversupply.
stories.summary: LCOR obtained $192.5 million in construction financing for a 39-story, 544-unit luxury apartment tower at 1775 Biscayne Boulevard in Edgewater, Miami, with a cost of $354,000 per unit. Natixis Corporate and Investment Banking led the syndicate; completion is targeted for Q4 2028. LCOR acquired the site in 2022 for $49 million and announced the project in 2023, proceeding despite South Florida's oversupply (18,600 units delivered in 2024, outpacing leasing by 20%).
stories.impact: South Florida multifamily supply glut persists but selective financing remains available for prime locations and experienced sponsors. Edgewater waterfront visibility and Related/LCOR pedigree attracted institutional lenders despite market oversupply. Implication: developers must compete on location (waterfront, walkability, amenity-dense submarkets) and sponsor strength; secondary/tertiary Miami locations will face financing headwinds. Supply overhang will suppress rents through 2025–2026 in non-flagship submarkets; capital should target infill Edgewater, Wynwood, Design District, or consider conversion/repositioning of distressed assets rather than greenfield class-A builds in weak-demand corridors.

stories.title: David Martin in talks to join Related, BH on Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment
stories.excerpt: Developer David Martin is in talks to join the Related Group and BH Group on their planned redevelopment of the Hollywood Beach Resort, sources told The Real Deal.  The Pérez family’s Coconut Grove-based Related and Isaac and Liat Toledano’s Aventura-based BH Group are under contract to acquire the 4.5-acre property at 101 North Ocean Drive, as previously reported by TRD. The Chetrit Group is selling the century-old resort. A source said the property is under contract for $100 million.  Martin, CEO of Terra Group declined to comment through a spokesperson. In a statement, BH named Terra and Related as its […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: David Martin (Terra Group) in talks to join Related Group + BH Group on Hollywood Beach Resort redevelopment.
Property: 4.5-acre oceanfront, 398-unit resort conversion; acquisition price ~$100M under contract.
Related/BH have 20+ projects together; Terra also developing with BH in West Palm Beach.
stories.summary: Terra Group CEO David Martin is negotiating to join the Related Group and BH Group in redeveloping the historic Hollywood Beach Resort, a 4.5-acre oceanfront property with 398 units currently owned by the Chetrit Group and under contract for approximately $100 million. BH and Related have an established track record with 20+ joint projects across South Florida and are also co-developing a luxury condo project in West Palm Beach. Terra's potential involvement signals consolidation among top-tier South Florida mixed-use developers.
stories.impact: South Florida: Continued consolidation of oceanfront conversion/repositioning work among Related, BH, and Terra suggests fragmented small-asset ownership is being rolled up into experienced mega-developers. This dynamic will limit land availability for smaller regional developers but signals strong capital appetite for beachfront adaptive reuse and condo/rental repositioning. Rents/sales prices on oceanfront may stabilize or grow faster than non-waterfront due to supply constraints and investor demand, creating a two-tier market in Miami/Fort Lauderdale.

stories.title: Whirlybird dreams: Ken Griffin wants helipad for Miami Beach megayacht marina
stories.excerpt: Billionaire Citadel founder Ken Griffin has already conquered the land and the sea in Miami Beach. His next target? The air.  City commissioners approved Griffin’s massive private megayacht marina at 120 MacArthur Causeway a few months ago, but now Griffin wants to chopper in. He submitted a request for a private, waterfront helipad at his sprawling Terminal Island development, the Miami Herald reported. The proposal, sponsored by Miami Beach Commissioner Joseph Magazine, is navigating the city’s Land Use and Sustainability Committee and the Planning Board. To clear the runway for Griffin, officials would need to amend Miami Beach’s land development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Ken Griffin (Citadel) seeking private helipad for Terminal Island megayacht marina (3.5-acre, Miami Beach).
Griffin acquired site from Related Group; adjacent to Coast Guard station; amends zoning for accessory use.
Griffin spent $169M assembling Star Island residential parcels; yacht (Defy) cost $175M in 2024.
stories.summary: Billionaire Ken Griffin is requesting approval for a private waterfront helipad at his Terminal Island megayacht marina development in Miami Beach, a 3.5-acre property he acquired from the Related Group. The proposal requires amending Miami Beach's land development regulations to permit private helipads as an accessory use in the industrial zoning district. Griffin has simultaneously invested $169 million assembling residential estates on Star Island nearby, but his 308-foot superyacht Defy is too large to moor at Star Island.
stories.impact: Minimal impact to multifamily development. This is a ultra-luxury niche amenity (private marina + helipad) reflecting Miami Beach's concentration of ultra-high-net-worth individuals and their ancillary real estate spending. No signal on residential absorption, rents, or supply dynamics; regulatory precedent for waterfront/industrial zoning variances may benefit future waterfront mixed-use developments, but this deal is luxury outlier, not market trend.

stories.title: Houston’s office glut is fueling a demolition trend for mostly vacant properties
stories.excerpt: As the Houston office market struggles to recover at the same rate as other similar cities, sometimes just tearing the buildings down is better for everyone involved.  Record low office vacancies and a need for private demolition has led to more demand than capital available for demolition work, according to Bisnow. Houston had an office vacancy rate of 26.8 percent, according to the outlet, a staggering figure attributable to old or obsolete office buildings and the pandemic ripple effect on employee hybrid schedules.  Vacant or near-vacant buildings all over the city can’t be demolished due to debt and lenders that […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Houston office vacancy 26.8%; demolition demand outstrips capital/capability; old/obsolete buildings being torn down.
Pandemic hybrid work and obsolete Class B/C stock driving negative absorption; Dallas recovering faster.
Owner-occupier conversions and out-of-distress sales increasing; maintenance costs on vacant buildings exceed redevelopment ROI.
stories.summary: Houston's office market is experiencing a demolition wave as record vacancy (26.8%) and pandemic-driven hybrid work trends make demolition cheaper than repositioning. Old, obsolete office buildings are being torn down faster than capital can be deployed; demolition companies report demand outstripping capability. Unlike peer cities (Dallas, Austin), Houston's office market is recovering slowly, with negative annual absorption and increased out-of-distress sales and owner-occupier conversions.
stories.impact: Houston market signal: Office-to-residential conversion economics may be attractive if demolition is an alternative. Developers should evaluate underutilized office parks and downtown office buildings as conversion candidates, especially in neighborhoods with strong residential demand (Midtown, Heights, EaDo). However, conversion capex (mechanical/systems, code compliance, parking) often exceeds demolition + new build; opportunity lies in selective older Class B/C buildings in walkable corridors with underlying land value sufficient to justify conversion risk. Office oversupply in Houston will depress commercial rents and increase distressed asset availability through 2025–2026, creating windows for land-stack conversions or lease buyouts.

stories.title: Inflationary Concerns Send School Shoppers Back to Discount Stores: Report
stories.excerpt: Parents’ back-to-school budgets are up nearly 12 percent this year, according to JLL’s 2026 Back-to-School Survey, and that’s good news for discount stores. The report, released Thursday, saw the dollar store sector enter the survey’s top 10 list of parents’ most-cited shopping destinations. The sector’s rising star comes as Americans continue to tighten belts in the face of inflation. The brokerage’s online survey collected responses from 1,022 U.S. parents with children between the ages of 5 and 18. Its release coincides with the July back-to-school shopping surge, when 27 percent of surveyed parents plan to check off their children’s classroom wish lists. Over 90 percent of parents plan to shop at physical stores this year, according to the survey, and dollar stores and mass merchants dominated their spending plans. The popularity of cheaper retailers such as Dollar Tree and Walmart are no surprise –– the report found school shopping budgets rising 11.7 percent from last year, up to an average of $489 per child. The uptick runs well ahead of the current U.S. inflation rate of around 4 percent. “Families are seeking retailers that respect their time and budgets, which explains why 68.7 percent cite saving money, and 35.1 percent prioritize finding the full school list in one place,” James Cook, JLL’s Americas director of retail research, said in a statement. The largest budget hike was found among middle-income families, who raised their spending expectations roughly 20 percent this year to $495 per child. The number of discretionary items on parents’ shopping lists fell in kind for the second consecutive year, meaning fewer fidget spinners and novelty pens will be landing in shopping carts. Consumers are shopping more strategically, the report said, with stores like Dollar General and Dollar Tree gaining ground for their discounts, and mass retailers like Walmart taking the top spot for their one-stop-shop convenience. Walmart popularity r
bullets: JLL back-to-school survey: parents' budgets up 11.7% to $489/child; dollar stores enter top-10 shopping destinations.
90%+ plan physical store shopping; discount retailers (Dollar Tree, Walmart) dominating amid inflation concerns.
Consumer spending shift to discount/value channels signals ongoing household budget pressure despite nominal wage growth.
stories.summary: JLL's 2026 Back-to-School Survey found parents' school shopping budgets rising 11.7% year-over-year to an average of $489 per child, but the spending is concentrated at discount retailers such as Dollar Tree and Walmart. Over 90% of surveyed parents plan to shop at physical stores, and dollar stores entered the survey's top 10 destinations for the first time. The shift reflects ongoing inflationary pressure on household budgets despite nominal spending increases.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. This retail consumer behavior snapshot suggests household financial stress persists despite nominal budget growth—a macro signal that may inform apartment demand (renters prioritizing budget/value, deferring household formation) but the article provides no rent, migration, or employment data specific to multifamily markets.

stories.title: CBRE Opens Hospitality-Inspired Office in Columbus
stories.excerpt: CBRE announced the opening of its new 19,500-square-foot office on the fifth floor at 10 W. Nationwide Blvd., a trophy office building within the Arena District owned and developed by Nationwide Realty Investors in downtown Columbus, Ohio. The firm has relocated from 200 Civic Center Drive. The new office is part of CBRE’s Workplace360 program, which showcases technology and a wide variety of collaborative spaces designed to support hybrid working. “As one of Columbus’s top trophy office buildings, 10 W. Nationwide Blvd. is an ideal home, and CBRE is proud to be its first tenant,” said CBRE’s Michael Copella, Executive Managing Director and Market Leader, Central Ohio. “The office design reflects the spirit of Columbus and its distinctive culture while highlighting the scope of CBRE’s capabilities.” New technology is installed throughout the office to support remote and in-person collaboration between employees. The office’s design reflects the unique character of Columbus, drawing inspiration from bespoke hotels and hospitality-focused experiences. The post CBRE Opens Hospitality-Inspired Office in Columbus appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CBRE relocates to 10 W. Nationwide Blvd., downtown Columbus; 19,500 SF, 5th floor, trophy office.
Building owned/developed by Nationwide Realty Investors; CBRE first tenant in Arena District.
Office design mimics hospitality/hotel experience; Workplace360 program supporting hybrid work.
stories.summary: CBRE relocated its Columbus, Ohio office to 19,500 square feet on the fifth floor of 10 W. Nationwide Boulevard, a trophy office building in the Arena District owned by Nationwide Realty Investors. The new office is the building's first tenant and features hospitality-inspired design and collaborative spaces aligned with CBRE's Workplace360 hybrid work program. The move from 200 Civic Center Drive signals confidence in downtown Columbus' office market and Arena District redevelopment.
stories.impact: Columbus office market: CBRE's flagship relocation to trophy downtown space is a positive signal for that submarket but offers no direct insight into multifamily demand or supply. However, the choice of Arena District signals regional corporate investment confidence in mixed-use downtown; developers should monitor upstream residential demand (student/young professional) linked to downtown office/entertainment clusters in secondary metros like Columbus.

stories.title: JLL Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Retail Center to Curbline Properties
stories.excerpt: JLL has secured the sale of Clybourn Center, a 33,140-square-foot unanchored strip center strategically located in Chicago’s Lincoln Park neighborhood. JLL’s Capital Market Investment and Sales Advisory team, representing the seller, was led by Managing Director Michael Nieder and Director Brian Page. Leasing efforts at the center have been spearheaded by Steve Schwartz and his team. The buyer was Curbline Properties. Clybourn Center is strategically positioned at 2029-2073 N. Clybourn Ave., across from Foundry Park, a transformational $1 billion-plus mixed-use redevelopment project. When complete, Foundry Park will deliver approximately 3,700 residential units, 350,000 square feet of office space, 420,000 square feet of retail space, 250,000 square feet of hospitality space, and 12 acres of open space. The 91.2% occupied property features 86 surface-level parking spaces, which is a rare amenity for street-front retail in this dense urban corridor. Clybourn Center’s tenant mix is anchored by medical and wellness uses, with 38% of the gross leasable area occupied by medical tenants. The post JLL Arranges Sale of Lincoln Park Retail Center to Curbline Properties appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL sold Clybourn Center (33,140 SF, Lincoln Park, Chicago) to Curbline Properties; unanchored strip retail.
Property 91.2% occupied; 38% GLA medical/wellness tenants; 86 surface parking spaces (rare in urban density).
Positioned near Foundry Park: $1B+ mixed-use redevelopment (3,700 residential units, 350K SF office, 420K SF retail).
stories.summary: JLL brokered the sale of Clybourn Center, a 33,140-square-foot unanchored retail strip in Chicago's Lincoln Park neighborhood, to Curbline Properties. The property is 91.2% occupied with a tenant mix anchored by medical and wellness uses (38% of GLA) and features 86 surface parking spaces, a rarity in dense urban corridors. It is positioned directly across from Foundry Park, a $1B+ mixed-use redevelopment expected to deliver 3,700 residential units, 350,000 SF of office, and 420,000 SF of retail.
stories.impact: Chicago submarket dynamic: Foundry Park's 3,700-unit residential pipeline will drive demographic influx and retail demand in Lincoln Park. Existing retail properties like Clybourn Center should see rent and occupancy upside as foot traffic and local population density increase. For multifamily developers, Foundry Park's retail/office mix signals blended-use development is viable in Chicago; the 3,700 units suggest strong absorption pipeline through 2028–2030 in the North Side. Competition for infill sites in Lincoln Park and Clybourn corridor will intensify; developers should secure land/entitlements before Foundry Park phases 1–2 stabilize.

stories.title: MCA Realty Acquires 138K-SF Seattle-Area Industrial Portfolio
stories.excerpt: MCA Realty, Inc., a real estate investment company based in California, announced the acquisition of West Valley Distribution Center, a three-building 138,296-square-foot multi-tenant industrial portfolio located in Kent, Washington. The firm acquired the property from Tamarisk West Valley LLC with its MCA Realty Industrial Growth Fund II. MCA now owns three industrial properties encompassing 268,843 square feet in the Seattle MSA. Newmark’s Andrew Briner, Aaron Banks and Kevin Shannon represented both MCA Realty and the seller in the transaction. The firm’s Taylor Hoff and Thad Mallory have been retained to oversee leasing at the property.MCA Realty will make significant exterior and interior improvements. It also plans to reconfigure the suites. “This acquisition reflects our view that the Kent Valley will remain an attractive location for industrial occupants,” said Tyler Mattox, Principal at MCA Realty. “The property’s infill location, durable tenant demand, and upside potential make it a compelling addition to our portfolio.” Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post MCA Realty Acquires 138K-SF Seattle-Area Industrial Portfolio appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: MCA Realty acquired West Valley Distribution Center (138K SF, 3-building portfolio, Kent, WA) from Tamarisk West Valley LLC.
MCA now owns 269K SF across Seattle MSA; planning exterior/interior improvements and suite reconfigurations.
Kent Valley positioning as infill industrial location with durable tenant demand.
stories.summary: MCA Realty, a California-based investment company, acquired West Valley Distribution Center, a 138,296-square-foot three-building industrial portfolio in Kent, Washington, from Tamarisk West Valley LLC. The acquisition brings MCA's total Seattle MSA industrial holdings to 268,843 square feet across three properties. MCA plans significant capital improvements and suite reconfigurations to drive upside; the Kent Valley location is positioned as an infill option with strong tenant demand and appreciation potential.
stories.impact: Minimal impact to multifamily. Industrial portfolio acquisition in secondary Seattle market (Kent) reflects investor confidence in Pacific Northwest logistics demand but does not signal housing demand, supply, or rent dynamics. No relevance to apartment development feasibility or capital availability.

stories.title: Rithm Capital Lands $515M Financing for Midtown Office Tower
stories.excerpt: Cushman & Wakefield and Newmark announced that their respective Equity, Debt & Structured Finance teams advised Rithm Capital in securing $515 million in fixed-rate financing for 31 W. 52nd St., a 29-story, 785,000-square-foot office tower in Midtown Manhattan. The Cushman & Wakefield team of Gideon Gil, Zach Kraft and Cecelia Galligan co-brokered the financing alongside Newmark’s Jordan Roeschlaub, Nick Scribani, Adam Spies, Adam Doneger, Tim Polglase, Dan Axelson and Jack Fenton. Led by Wells Fargo, the financing package consists of a $415-million senior mortgage, a $40 million B-Note and a $60-million mezzanine loan. The lending group also includes Bank of America, Barclays, Citi, Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan. The refinancing follows Rithm Capital’s acquisition of the larger Paramount Group office portfolio. “Securing financing of this scale from a consortium of leading lenders demonstrates continued confidence in well-positioned office assets in New York City’s premier business districts,” said Gil. “As capital markets continue to evolve, institutional lenders remain focused on high-quality properties backed by experienced ownership and strong long-term fundamentals.” The post Rithm Capital Lands $515M Financing for Midtown Office Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Rithm Capital secures $515M fixed-rate refinancing for 31 W. 52nd St. (29-story, 785K SF, Midtown Manhattan office).
Senior $415M + $40M B-Note + $60M mezzanine; Wells Fargo led consortium (Bank of America, Barclays, Citi, Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan).
Rithm acquired larger Paramount Group office portfolio; refinancing follows consolidation.
stories.summary: Rithm Capital obtained $515 million in fixed-rate financing for 31 West 52nd Street, a 29-story, 785,000-square-foot office tower in Midtown Manhattan, comprising a $415 million senior mortgage, $40 million B-Note, and $60 million mezzanine loan. A consortium of seven major lenders (Wells Fargo, BofA, Barclays, Citi, Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan) participated. The refinancing follows Rithm Capital's acquisition of the larger Paramount Group office portfolio and demonstrates continued lender appetite for premier Manhattan office assets.
stories.impact: New York office market signal: Major lenders remain disciplined but active in Tier-1 trophy Manhattan office backed by strong sponsors. The $515M refinance (~$656/SF) suggests prime Midtown office is stabilizing at constructive valuations; however, this deal reflects institutional mega-sponsor activity (Rithm/Paramount), not broader market recovery. Secondary/tertiary Manhattan office remains distressed. No direct multifamily impact; however, office weakness in Manhattan may indirectly support conversion economics if underutilized Class B/C office towers in secondary locations become acquisition targets for conversion to residential, competing for capital with new apartment construction.

stories.title: Auto Parts Distributor Signs $42M Lease in Southern California
stories.excerpt: An auto parts distribution company is expanding in Southern California and has signed a lease to take over a 522,267-square-foot industrial building in the Inland Empire. The lease agreement with landlord Invesco is valued at more than $42 million, according to Daum Commercial Real Estate Services, which announced the deal Thursday and represented the tenant alongside Lara CRE. Daum said the lease represents a significant expansion of the tenant’s Southern California distribution footprint, but did not disclose the company’s name or the length of the lease. The property at 16380 Euclid Avenue is Building 3 at the Chino South Industrial Center in Chino, Calif. It was constructed in 2014 with 32-foot clear heights and dock-high loading bays. Chino’s direct industrial vacancy was at 3.24 percent in the first quarter with limited availability for large distribution space, according to Daum’s research. Daum’s Gus Andros represented the tenant along with Nathan Lara, Andrew Lara, Rudy Lara and Jack Nersesian of Lara CRE. Steve Bellitti, Tom Taylor, Joey Jones and Evelyn Manning of Colliers represented Invesco. Colliers did not immediately return a request for comment. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Auto parts distributor signs $42M lease for 522K SF building (Chino, CA, Inland Empire industrial).
Lease represents significant tenant expansion in Southern California distribution network; property built 2014, 32-ft clear heights.
Chino direct industrial vacancy 3.24% Q1; limited large distribution space availability.
stories.summary: An undisclosed auto parts distribution company signed a lease valued at over $42 million for a 522,267-square-foot industrial building (Building 3, Chino South Industrial Center) in Chino, California. The lease represents a significant expansion of the tenant's Southern California distribution footprint. The property, built in 2014 with 32-foot clear heights and dock-high loading, is in a tight Inland Empire submarket with direct industrial vacancy at just 3.24% in Q1, limiting large distribution space availability.
stories.impact: Inland Empire industrial supply remains tight, supporting rents and occupancy in logistics/distribution submarkets (Chino, San Bernardino, Riverside). However, no direct relevance to multifamily development. Continued industrial demand and tight vacancies in Inland Empire suggest strong regional employment and population; this may support apartment demand in communities like Ontario, Fontana, and Moreno Valley, but the article provides no housing market data to confirm absorption or rent trends.
